item.title: CalPhishing, The Gentlemen Ransomware, Miasma/Hades, and AI Agent OAuth Risk
item.description: This digest window is dominated by social engineering techniques moving beyond conventional email, expanding ransomware operations, and renewed concern over software supply chain exposure on developer systems. Reporting also highlights how AI agents and autonomous workflows are changing trust...
item.content:encoded:
This digest window is dominated by social engineering techniques moving beyond conventional email, expanding ransomware operations, and renewed concern over software supply chain exposure on developer systems. Reporting also highlights how AI agents and autonomous workflows are changing trust boundaries around OAuth, prompt injection, and managed security operations. Health-ISAC reported an emerging healthcare-sector social engineering pattern called CalPhishing, in which attackers use iCalendar files and enterprise calendar auto-processing to place malicious links directly into employee schedules. Recent campaigns reportedly use ConsentFix, also described as device code phishing, to capture session tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication protections after the user interaction stage. Netskope Threat Labs framed OAuth authorization abuse as a structural scaling problem, particularly as organizations adopt AI agents that rely on persistent tokens, broad permissions, opaque runtimes, and autonomous workflows. The article described a confused deputy scenario in which an attacker plants malicious instructions in data consumed by an agent, causing the agent to use its own valid OAuth token to perform attacker-directed actions. SpecterOps published research on automating indirect prompt injection testing against an agentic system with access to Notion search, shell execution, and web-fetching tools. The workflow used poisoned Notion content to progress from simple echo payloads to full-chain payloads involving command execution and HTTP exfiltration, while noting that newer Claude Sonnet model behavior detected some earlier payload styles more reliably. StepSecurity reported that the Miasma worm and its PyPI branch Hades are spreading across npm and PyPI ecosystems, with execution paths tied to project opening, package installation, or package import. Miasma uses a small binding.gyp file in a technique described as “Phantom Gyp,” while Hades relies on an obfuscated import hook in init.py, creating visibility gaps for tools focused primarily on install-time checks, process behavior, or declared dependencies. Aikido highlighted npm v12, scheduled for July 2026, as a major package-manager security change because dependency install scripts will no longer run by default unless packages are allowlisted. The release is also expected to block Git dependencies and remote URL dependencies unless explicitly permitted, and to treat implicit node-gyp rebuilds triggered by binding.gyp as install scripts. Socket announced Andrew Becherer as its first Chief Information Security Officer, positioning the appointment around enterprise concerns with open source, AI tooling, and malicious package risk. The company said it now protects more than 27,000 organizations and framed the role as both an internal security function and a customer-facing software supply chain security leadership position. PRODAFT reporting cited by The Hacker News tracks The Gentlemen ransomware operation as Phantom Mantis and says the group shifted from affiliate activity into an independent partnership program in July 2025. The operation has claimed 478 victims, reportedly accounted for 10% of ransomware activity in April 2026, and includes Go-based ransomware that Microsoft tracks under Storm-2697 with a self-propagation mode when launched with the --spread argument. Law enforcement dismantled AudiA6, a cryptocurrency service Europol said was used by ransomware actors and other cybercriminals to launder more than $380 million. Authorities arrested two individuals in Georgia, seized domains, vehicles, properties, cryptocurrency assets, Telegram accounts, and retrieved 6,000 Know-Your-Customer records tied to alleged money mule accounts. In the U.K., Great Marlow School in Buckinghamshire sent most students home for a second consecutive day after a cybersecurity incident affected ICT systems, while students taking GCSE and A-Level exams were allowed onsite. The incident type was not confirmed, but the reporting cited nearly 2,000 education and childcare-sector cyber incidents recorded by the U.K. Information Commissioner’s Office between 2019 and 2025. Russian national Denis Obrezko appeared in U.S. federal court after transfer from Thailand, where he was arrested in November, over allegations that he supported the Russian state-linked Void Blizzard group. Prosecutors allege cryptocurrency transactions linked to Obrezko funded infrastructure used in attacks, and an FBI affidavit identified at least 11 compromised U.S. companies while suggesting the actual victim count is higher. In U.S. defense policy, a Senate Armed Services Committee amendment to create a standalone Cyber Force failed by a 14-13 vote. The debate reflected bipartisan frustration over cyber personnel readiness for U.S. Cyber Command, while the Senate bill separately proposed a new Under Secretary of Defense for Cyber, Information, and Networks, dual-hatted as the Pentagon CIO and principal cyber advisor.

The Gentlemen Ransomware Claims 478 Victims, Can Spread Like a Worm — Should Read

The Gentlemen ransomware group has rapidly grown into a major RaaS operation with 478 claimed victims, evolving from affiliate activity into an independent program led by a Russian-speaking actor tied to prior ransomware crews. Its Go-based ransomware can spread worm-like across networks, supports multiple platforms, and is backed by mature affiliate support, BYOVD evasion, and aggressive double-extortion tactics.

  • PRODAFT tracks The Gentlemen operation as Phantom Mantis and says it transitioned in July 2025 from an affiliate using other RaaS resources into an independent partnership program.
  • The Gentlemen has claimed 478 victims to date, and reports cited in the article say it accounted for 10% of ransomware activity in April 2026.
  • Microsoft tracks the cluster as Storm-2697 and says its Go-based ransomware can become self-propagating across reachable network systems when run with the --spread argument.
  • The group provides Windows, Linux, ESXi, Windows XP+, and Logical Volume Manager ransomware versions and offers affiliates a 90% profit share.
  • The article says The Gentlemen uses tactics and tools including BYOVD-based EDR killing, vulnerable edge-device access, red team utilities, log clearing, Microsoft Defender disabling, and multi-channel extortion.

Miasma and Hades Are Spreading Now: Detect Them on Developer Machines with Suspicious Files — Should Read

Miasma and Hades are fast-spreading npm and PyPI worms that execute from project files at open or import time, bypassing many install-time defenses while stealing developer secrets and publishing tokens. StepSecurity is promoting Dev Machine Guard’s Suspicious Files feature to detect the specific files these campaigns drop across developer machines.

  • The Miasma worm and its PyPI branch Hades are spreading across the npm and PyPI ecosystems and can run when a developer opens a project or imports a package.
  • Miasma uses a “Phantom Gyp” technique involving a small binding.gyp file to trigger code execution during npm install, while Hades executes through an obfuscated hook in a package’s __init__.py file on import.
  • The article states that registry gateways, EDR, and SCA scanners can miss these campaigns because they focus on install-time checks, process behavior, or declared dependencies rather than suspicious files on developer machines.
  • StepSecurity’s Suspicious Files feature detects files associated with known supply chain attacks across enrolled developer machines using centrally maintained detection rules.
  • The feature currently covers Miasma and Hades artifacts such as malicious binding.gyp files, injected import hooks, and editor or AI-tool configuration files that can auto-execute when projects are opened.

Authorities dismantle 'AudiA6' ransomware crypto-laundering service — Should Read

Authorities dismantled AudiA6, a cryptocurrency laundering service allegedly used by ransomware gangs and other cybercriminals to wash more than $380 million. Two alleged administrators were arrested in Georgia, with domains, assets, crypto, and Telegram accounts seized or frozen.

  • Law enforcement dismantled the AudiA6 cryptocurrency service, which Europol says was used by ransomware actors and other cybercriminals to launder more than $380 million.
  • Europol said AudiA6 was linked to more than 15 international investigations involving ransomware attacks and large-scale cryptocurrency theft.
  • Authorities arrested two individuals in Georgia, seized 25 domains, seized 80 vehicles and properties, seized €86,000 in cryptocurrency, froze €692,000 in cryptocurrency, and blocked Telegram accounts used by the network.
  • The U.S. Department of Justice named Ruslan Igorevich Tkachuk and Alexander Vladimirovich Ledenev as senior members of the AudiA6 platform.
  • Authorities also retrieved 6,000 Know-Your-Customer records linked to money mule accounts allegedly created using stolen or purchased identities.

Hacker linked to Void Blizzard faces charges over cyberespionage campaign — Should Read

Russian national Denis Obrezko appeared in U.S. federal court on charges that he provided infrastructure supporting Void Blizzard, a Kremlin-linked cyberespionage group accused of compromising at least 11 U.S. companies. He was arrested in Thailand and transferred to U.S. custody as prosecutors allege his crypto-linked purchases helped facilitate attacks against organizations in the U.S. and abroad.

  • Russian national Denis Obrezko appeared in U.S. federal court in Boston after being transferred from Thailand, where he was arrested in November.
  • U.S. prosecutors allege that Obrezko supported the Russian state-linked Void Blizzard hacking group by providing infrastructure used in cyber operations.
  • Prosecutors allege that cryptocurrency transactions linked to Obrezko were used to purchase a virtual private server and internet domain that facilitated attacks against organizations in the United States and other countries.
  • An FBI affidavit says investigators identified at least 11 compromised U.S. companies, though authorities believe the actual number of victims is significantly higher.
  • Researchers have described Void Blizzard as a relatively new threat group supporting Russian government interests and targeting organizations across Europe and North America.

npm v12 delivers one of the biggest security improvements in years — Should Read

npm v12 will stop running dependency install scripts by default, blocking a major supply-chain attack path abused by recent npm worms and credential stealers. Teams should test npm 11.16.0 warnings now, approve only trusted install scripts, and prepare for the v12 default change.

  • npm v12, scheduled for July 2026, will stop running dependency install scripts by default and will only run scripts for packages placed on an allowlist.
  • The change targets supply chain attacks that abuse preinstall, install, and postinstall scripts, including incidents such as Nx s1ngularity, Shai-Hulud, the axios hijack, and Mini Shai-Hulud.
  • npm v12 will also block Git dependencies unless --allow-git is used and remote-URL dependencies unless --allow-remote is used.
  • npm v12 treats implicit node-gyp rebuilds triggered by binding.gyp as install scripts, so those builds will also be blocked unless the package is allowlisted.
  • The article recommends upgrading to npm 11.16.0 or later, using npm approve-scripts --allow-scripts-pending to identify packages that would be blocked, and committing the resulting package.json changes.

The Rise of CalPhishing Attacks in the Health Sector — Should Read

Health-ISAC warns that attackers are using malicious calendar invites and .ics files to place phishing links directly into Outlook and Google Calendar events, where reminders can persist even after the original email is deleted. Healthcare organizations should disable automatic processing of external invites, inspect calendar files, restrict device-code authentication, and ensure malicious events are hard-deleted.

  • Health-ISAC observed an emerging social engineering threat called CalPhishing, or Calendar Phishing, on June 11, 2026.
  • Threat actors exploit automatic processing features in enterprise calendar applications such as Microsoft Outlook and Google Calendar to deliver malicious links into employees’ schedules.
  • CalPhishing uses iCalendar (.ics) files containing phishing URLs and credential-harvesting mechanisms to bypass traditional email security gateways.
  • Recent campaigns use ConsentFix, also described as device code phishing, to capture session tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication.
  • Health-ISAC recommends disabling automatic processing of external calendar invites, inspecting .ics files, restricting device code authentication flows, hard-deleting malicious calendar artifacts, and training staff to recognize calendar phishing.

Cyber Force not included in Senate defense policy roadmap — May Read

A Senate Armed Services Committee amendment to create a U.S. Cyber Force narrowly failed, with lawmakers opting to wait for a pending feasibility study despite bipartisan concerns that existing military services are not adequately staffing U.S. Cyber Command. The defense bill instead advances a major Pentagon cyber office reorganization to reduce friction between cyber policy and CIO responsibilities.

  • An amendment by Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand to create a U.S. Cyber Force in the Senate’s fiscal 2027 defense policy bill was defeated 14-13 in the Senate Armed Services Committee.
  • Opponents argued that lawmakers should wait for a National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine feasibility study on creating a Cyber Force, which is expected to conclude later this year.
  • The close vote reflected bipartisan frustration over the existing military services’ failure to provide U.S. Cyber Command with personnel ready for online operations against adversaries such as China and Russia.
  • An independent commission recently reported that creating a seventh military branch for cyber would cost up to $11 billion and include an estimated 33,000 troops.
  • The Senate defense bill would create a new Under Secretary of Defense for Cyber, Information, and Networks, dual-hatted as the Pentagon’s Chief Information Officer and principal cyber advisor to the Secretary of Defense.

British high school sends students home following cyberattack — May Read

Great Marlow School in Buckinghamshire sent most students home for a second day after a cybersecurity incident disrupted its ICT systems, with only exam students allowed on site. The incident highlights continued cyber disruption facing schools in the UK and abroad, though the nature of this attack has not been confirmed.

  • Great Marlow School in Buckinghamshire, England, sent most students home for a second consecutive day after a cybersecurity incident affected its ICT systems.
  • The school said only students sitting GCSE and A-Level external examinations were permitted to attend while it worked with specialist IT and cybersecurity professionals to resolve the issue.
  • Headteacher Guy Pendlebury said the school was responding to the incident in line with guidance from the Department for Education and the National Cyber Security Centre.
  • The UK's Information Commissioner's Office recorded 1,959 cyber incidents affecting the education and childcare sector between 2019 and 2025.
  • The nature of the incident affecting Great Marlow School has not been confirmed.

Building an Indirect Prompt Injection Workflow — May Read

The article shows how the author automated indirect prompt injection testing against an agentic AI system, using Codex to generate, test, and refine payloads that could trigger tool use, command execution, and HTTP exfiltration. The key takeaway is that newer models improved detection, but iterative AI-driven payload refinement still found reliable bypasses.

  • The author built an automated workflow using OpenAI Codex to generate, inject, test, evaluate, and refine indirect prompt injection payloads against an agentic system using Claude Sonnet models on Amazon Bedrock.
  • The target agent had access to Notion search, shell command execution, and web page fetching tools, and the author used a poisoned Notion page as the indirect prompt injection point.
  • Manual testing showed that simple echo payloads worked more easily than complex payloads involving base64 encoding, command execution, or HTTP exfiltration, which often triggered detection or were ignored.
  • The automated workflow progressed through staged testing from echo validation to full-chain payloads that encoded responses, ran `whoami`, and exfiltrated data via HTTP requests.
  • The author found that Claude Sonnet 4.6 detected some Sonnet 4.5 payloads more reliably, but revised payloads using softer language and different formatting still achieved high success rates in the test environment.

AI Agents and the OAuth Trust Problem at Scale — Optional

AI agents can turn long-lived, over-scoped OAuth tokens into a massive enterprise attack surface that bypasses MFA and enables authorized-looking abuse at scale. Organizations should inventory agent tokens and move toward short-lived, task-scoped, observable credentials with strong governance.

  • Netskope Threat Labs argues that OAuth authorization abuse has become a structural security problem as tokens can continue operating silently after a user’s initial MFA-backed consent.
  • The article estimates that 1,000 employees using five AI agents each could create exposure from about 50,000 active OAuth tokens, many of which may be long-lived and rarely reviewed.
  • AI agents worsen OAuth risk because they may use persistent tokens, broad scopes, opaque runtimes, autonomous actions, and arbitrary execution workflows.
  • The article describes a confused deputy scenario in which an attacker plants malicious instructions in data an agent consumes, causing the agent to use its own valid token to carry out the attacker’s intent.
  • Netskope recommends governance based on isolation, short-lived tokens, strict scoping, segmentation, observability, and detection and response capabilities for agent OAuth usage.

Andrew Becherer Joins Socket as Chief Information Security Officer — Optional

Socket has appointed Andrew Becherer as its first Chief Information Security Officer to lead security, compliance, and risk as the company scales its software supply chain security work. Becherer brings prior CISO experience from Datadog, Iterable, and Sublime Security.

  • Socket has appointed Andrew Becherer as its first Chief Information Security Officer.
  • Socket says it now protects more than 27,000 organizations, including enterprises that rely on it to secure software supply chains.
  • Andrew Becherer previously served as CISO at Datadog, Iterable, and Sublime Security, and founded Staris AI between Iterable and Sublime.
  • Socket CEO Feross Aboukhadijeh said Becherer will own how Socket protects itself and how it supports the security teams it serves.
  • Becherer said he joined Socket because software supply chain security is a critical problem for CISOs trying to balance open source and AI tooling with malicious package risk.

Why AI-driven threats are exposing the limits of MSP security stacks — Optional

AI-driven attacks are shrinking response windows and exposing the weaknesses of fragmented MSP security stacks. The article argues MSPs need unified, automated security platforms to improve detection, response, recovery, and scalability.

  • AI is accelerating cybercrime by enabling faster phishing, reconnaissance, initial access, malware development, and automated vulnerability exploitation.
  • The article says MSPs using fragmented security stacks lose time switching between disconnected tools for EDR alerts, backup verification, patching data, and remediation validation.
  • Modern endpoint security operations need fast detection, coordinated response, and rapid recovery across integrated workflows.
  • The article argues that automation and AI-assisted response can help MSPs patch vulnerabilities, enforce policies, detect anomalies, and trigger remediation without waiting for technicians.
  • Kaseya says unified platforms such as Kaseya 365 Endpoint can combine RMM, endpoint security, patch management, backup, ransomware protection, MDR, and SOC services into one operational model.

item.title: ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, Coupang Fine, LangGraph CVEs, and AI Agent Risks
item.description: This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of...
item.content:encoded: This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of phishing and vishing tactics that continue to rely on trusted brands and user interaction. The University of Nottingham confirmed a cyber incident involving external access to a significant amount of data affecting current and former students. ShinyHunters claimed on its extortion site that it obtained more than 40GB of material, including payment-related data, while HaveIBeenPwned analysis of partially published data identified roughly 455,000 unique email addresses and associated personal, academic, and fee information. In South Korea, Coupang received a record 624.6 billion won, approximately $409 million, fine from the Personal Information Protection Commission after a breach affecting about 37.55 million people. The regulator cited authentication signature key management failures, access control deficiencies, data destruction and notification violations, and obstruction of the investigation; authorities identified a former Coupang IT employee, a 43-year-old Chinese national, as the primary suspect. Multiple reports emphasized that attackers are increasingly operating through identity systems rather than relying solely on traditional exploit chains. Flashpoint reported more than 11.1 million devices infected with infostealers last year, producing over 3.3 billion stolen credentials, cookies, cloud tokens, and other identity artifacts circulating in criminal markets. AI agent security emerged as a recurring concern across SaaS, developer workflows, and CI/CD environments. Varonis found that the OpenClaw email agent Pinchy could be tricked by ordinary-looking phishing emails into forwarding sensitive material, while Microsoft disclosed and Anthropic patched a Claude Code GitHub Action issue that could expose workflow secrets when AI agents processed untrusted GitHub content. AppOmni described agentic AI as a new class of SaaS identity capable of fetching data, creating users, and modifying configurations. Related identity exposure analysis from XM Cyber highlighted the risk of unmanaged human, machine, service, cloud, and AI accounts across hybrid environments, particularly where credentials are reused, cached, weak, or poorly governed. Check Point Research disclosed three vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing implementations, including CVE-2025-67644, a SQLite checkpointer SQL injection, and CVE-2026-28277, an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue. The research showed that SQL injection could be chained with unsafe deserialization to achieve remote code execution where applications expose getstatehistory() with attacker-controlled filters; fixes are available in updated LangGraph and checkpoint packages. CISA announced Binding Operational Directive 26-04, requiring U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to remediate high-risk vulnerabilities under accelerated timelines, with the shortest deadline set at three days. Prioritization is based on factors including public exposure, Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog status, automation potential, and whether exploitation grants partial or full system control. The broader vulnerability management discussion reflected shrinking exploit windows. The Hacker News cited reporting that AI-assisted discovery has increased vulnerability volume and reduced average time-to-exploit, while Aikido’s Docker Hardened Images integration illustrated parallel efforts to reduce container vulnerability noise through signed SBOMs and Docker VEX attestations. IRONSCALES analyzed an image-only callback phishing email posing as a Geek Squad renewal invoice for $270. The message used an empty subject line, a JPEG attachment, and a callback number, with OCR revealing a brand mismatch between Geek Squad and “Windows Defender” copyright text. Industrial security and industry community activity also featured in the window. Radiflow and Barrier announced an OT security networking dinner in Aberdeen focused on cyber resilience, industrial threat visibility, and critical infrastructure security, while The Hacker News published the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners across 95 subcategories.

item.title: Ivanti CVE-2026-10520 Exploitation, ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, and npm Supply Chain Controls
item.description: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability updates affecting BitLocker recovery behavior and continued attention to operational technology visibility gaps. Attackers are reportedly targeting CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw in Ivanti Sentry that enables code execution with root privileges on Internet-exposed secure mobile gateways. Ivanti patched the vulnerability in Sentry versions R10.5.2, R10.6.2, and R10.7.1, but Shadowserver reported soon after that exposed vulnerable systems had already been backdoored in some cases. Shadowserver observed 19 vulnerable Ivanti Sentry instances during scanning and said at least two were backdoored, warning that unpatched exposed systems were likely compromised. The report adds to a broader Ivanti risk pattern: CISA has flagged 34 vulnerabilities across Ivanti products as actively exploited in the wild, including 12 that were also targeted in ransomware attacks. The University of Nottingham confirmed unauthorized access to its student records system, affecting current students and alumni. Have I Been Pwned reported that 454,600 individuals were impacted, with exposed data including email addresses, names, addresses, phone numbers, ethnicities, disabilities, passport numbers, academic enrolment information, and fee payment information. The ShinyHunters extortion gang claimed responsibility and said it stole more than 40GB of documents from the University of Nottingham and its Malaysia and China campuses. The university said it is working with the third party that maintains the affected platform on a forensic investigation and has reported the incident to Action Fraud and the UK Information Commissioner’s Office. BleepingComputer linked the intrusion to a wider ShinyHunters campaign targeting cloud and on-premises Oracle PeopleSoft instances at more than 100 organizations globally. The incident highlights continued actor interest in enterprise student information systems and third-party-maintained platforms containing high-volume personal and administrative records. GitHub announced breaking changes planned for npm version 12 that will disable dependency install scripts by default. The change targets a long-standing supply chain risk in which npm install can execute preinstall, install, and postinstall scripts from transitive dependencies, allowing a compromised package anywhere in the dependency tree to run arbitrary code on developer systems or CI runners. Under the npm 12 model, dependency install scripts will not execute unless explicitly approved at the project level. npm install will also stop resolving Git dependencies or dependencies from remote URLs unless those behaviors are explicitly allowed with relevant options, narrowing default execution and resolution pathways commonly abused in dependency-chain compromise scenarios. Microsoft also resolved a Windows platform issue that caused some Windows Server 2025 systems to boot into BitLocker recovery after the April 2026 security update. The bug affected devices with BitLocker enabled on the OS drive and a specific unrecommended Group Policy configuration involving TPM platform validation and PCR7; fixes were released in KB5094125 for Windows Server 2025 and KB5093998 for Windows 11 23H2. A webinar titled “Closing the OT Security Visibility Gap: From Blind Spots to Continuous Protection” focuses on persistent visibility limitations in operational technology environments. The framing reflects a broader security concern around insufficient asset, traffic, and behavioral visibility across industrial networks where continuous monitoring remains harder than in conventional IT environments. The topic aligns with ongoing pressure on defenders to understand OT exposure and detect changes in environments that often include legacy systems, limited instrumentation, and operational constraints. While the article is webinar-focused rather than incident-driven, it reflects continued industry emphasis on closing monitoring gaps in critical infrastructure and industrial settings.

item.title: MCP Security Tradeoffs for Agentic SOC Access
item.description: This digest centers on the operational and security implications of Model Context Protocol (MCP) adoption in security environments. The reporting frames MCP not as a replacement for APIs, but as a parallel access model designed for AI agents and analyst-driven investigation workflows, with new...
item.content:encoded: This digest centers on the operational and security implications of Model Context Protocol (MCP) adoption in security environments. The reporting frames MCP not as a replacement for APIs, but as a parallel access model designed for AI agents and analyst-driven investigation workflows, with new implications for credential exposure and access control. Mitiga’s analysis distinguishes APIs and MCP by their intended consumers. APIs are described as contracts for code, best suited to deterministic automation where developers know which endpoints to call and require predictable behavior. MCP, by contrast, is presented as a contract for AI agents that need to discover, select, and chain tools during live investigative tasks. The article positions APIs as better aligned with scripted workflows, integrations, and repeatable automation. MCP is framed as more useful for analysts, AI assistants, and Agentic SOC platforms operating through natural language prompts, where the agent determines which tools to use and how to combine outputs during an investigation. A key reported benefit of MCP is reduced time-to-first-answer in security investigations. The article states that MCP can allow an LLM-driven system to choose tools, chain results, and format outputs without requiring a pre-built workflow, potentially compressing early investigative steps into minutes. The same access model also expands the security surface of AI-enabled SOC operations. Mitiga notes that MCP servers provide agents with credentialed access into security environments, making MCP tokens, scopes, and server permissions part of the effective attack surface. In this framing, MCP adoption introduces identity and authorization considerations similar to API security, but applied to autonomous or semi-autonomous agent activity. The dominant theme is the coexistence of deterministic automation and agentic investigation models. APIs remain central for predictable machine-to-machine execution, while MCP is presented as an emerging interface layer for AI-assisted security operations that can accelerate analysis while concentrating privileged access paths around agent tooling.

item.title: Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, Miasma Leak, and PeopleSoft ShinyHunters Attacks
item.description: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware,...
item.content:encoded: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware, and defensive changes planned for npm in response to recent package ecosystem abuse. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-5027, a high-severity path traversal flaw in Langflow that allows arbitrary file writes on exposed servers. The issue affects the POST /api/v2/files endpoint, where the filename parameter in multipart form data is not properly sanitized. Tenable disclosed the vulnerability on March 27, 2026, after reporting it to Langflow more than two months earlier without receiving a response; Snyk reported fixes in langflow-base 0.8.3 and Langflow 1.9.0, with 1.10.0 listed as the latest release. Separately, ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for ongoing data theft attacks against Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises customer instances. The group claimed to have stolen data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations, with education reportedly making up most of the affected sector. The actor described its exploitation path as a “gadget chain” involving old and zero-day vulnerabilities, with impact dependent on instance configuration. Researcher Michael R identified exposed online directories containing apparent attack tooling related to the PeopleSoft activity, including MeshCentral agents, a defacement and credential spray script, and staging materials. The two cases underscore continued attacker focus on externally reachable enterprise platforms where file write, deserialization, or configuration-dependent exploit chains can quickly become data theft or persistence opportunities. The Miasma credential-stealing framework was briefly exposed through GitHub repositories named “Miasma-Open-Source-Release” across numerous compromised developer accounts. SafeDep assessed that Miasma appears to be an evolution of the earlier Shai-Hulud worm, sharing many features, techniques, and code. The framework steals build environment and cloud credentials, compromises legitimate repositories and packages, and publishes trojanized versions to reach downstream developers. A notable feature of Miasma is its use of GitHub itself for command-and-control-like functions, reducing dependence on separate infrastructure. The leaked code also revealed a dead-man switch capable of deleting files in a user’s home and Documents folders if a stolen GitHub token used for exfiltration is revoked. This behavior combines credential theft, repository abuse, and destructive pressure against victims attempting to cut off attacker access. GitHub announced npm v12 security changes intended to disrupt similar supply-chain tactics. In npm v12, npm install will not run dependency preinstall, install, or postinstall scripts unless explicitly approved, and it will no longer fetch dependencies from Git repositories or remote URLs such as HTTPS tarballs unless permitted. GitHub said the defaults could have disrupted techniques seen in malicious install scripts, data-stealing npm packages, and Git dependency abuse documented in Shai-Hulud attacks. JFrog’s discussion of IDC’s 2026 Software Engineering Challenges Report framed Shadow AI as a growing unmanaged enterprise risk, replacing Shadow IT as developers use personal accounts, unvetted plugins, and AI-generated code in production workflows. The report, based on a survey of 1,000 enterprise and mid-sized organizations, described a widening gap between AI adoption goals and governance readiness. The article argued that AI agents cannot be assumed to self-police compliance because their behavior is non-deterministic and may bypass policies that exist only as documentation or informal agreements. Aikido described a parallel shift in where code is written, citing PostHog’s observation that roughly as many pull requests are being merged through Slack as through the code editor. The article said developers increasingly write code through AI agents, chat interfaces, and MCP servers that may be outside security team visibility. It connected this dispersion to recent supply-chain incidents such as TeamPCP activity, the Axios maintainer account hijacking, and the Glassworm attack, which emphasized compromise of developer devices and accounts rather than application vulnerabilities. Developer endpoints were characterized as high-value control points because they often hold cloud credentials, SSH keys, npm publish tokens, Kubernetes configs, source code access, and production access. The reporting also noted limitations in traditional MDM and EDR visibility for malicious packages, IDE extensions, browser plugins, and postinstall hooks before credential theft occurs. CISA issued a new binding operational directive requiring U.S. federal civilian agencies to patch certain high-risk vulnerabilities within 72 hours. The accelerated timeline applies when vulnerabilities meet three of four criteria: public internet exposure, presence in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, automated exploitability, and the degree of adversary control over the vulnerable system. Agencies have 180 days to adopt the new time frame. The directive also requires agencies to assess when and how a vulnerable system may have been compromised before patching, reflecting CISA’s position that applying a patch does not generally evict an existing threat actor. CISA also urged state, tribal, and local governments, along with critical infrastructure owners and operators, to adopt similar practices.

item.title: JDY Botnet, Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, and Critical Ivanti/Fortinet/SAP Patches
item.description: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive cyberattack affecting Australian sugar production. Multiple articles focused on how AI agents challenge existing zero trust and security inspection models. Varonis and Netskope both described AI agents as non-human actors that authenticate, call APIs, access databases, invoke tools, and exchange data at machine speed, creating gaps in policies designed around human access and authentication-time controls. Reported threat scenarios included prompt injection, tool poisoning, identity and privilege abuse, memory poisoning, supply chain compromise, and uninspected Model Context Protocol or app-to-LLM traffic. Software supply chain exposure in AI-assisted development also featured prominently. Socket announced an integration with Replit intended to evaluate open source packages as they enter AI-powered build workflows, citing threats such as typosquatting, impersonated packages, malicious transitive dependencies, credential stealers, and install scripts that retrieve second-stage payloads. The reporting reflects a broader shift in which AI agents can recommend, install, and wire dependencies directly into applications. SpecterOps published research on SQL Server 2025 AI-related features, demonstrating offensive use cases involving spinvokeexternalrestendpoint, CREATE EXTERNAL MODEL, and AIGENERATEEMBEDDINGS. The proof-of-concept activity included HTTPS exfiltration of table contents and files, trigger-based persistence for newly inserted data, NTLM coercion over SMB via UNC paths for ONNX models, and command-and-control patterns disguised as embedding-style traffic. Langflow CVE-2026-5027 stood out because it is reported as unpatched and under active exploitation. Tenable described the flaw as a path traversal issue in the POST /api/v2/files endpoint caused by insufficient sanitization of multipart filename values, while VulnCheck reported that default unauthenticated auto-login can allow attackers to obtain a session token and reach remote code execution. Censys data cited in the reporting identified roughly 7,000 internet-exposed Langflow instances, with most in North America. Patch reporting covered several critical enterprise products. Fortinet fixed CVE-2026-25089, a CVSS 9.1 command injection flaw in FortiSandbox products; Ivanti fixed CVE-2026-10520, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated root-level command injection issue in Ivanti Sentry, and CVE-2026-10523, a CVSS 9.9 authentication bypass enabling creation of arbitrary administrative accounts. SAP also released fixes for critical vulnerabilities affecting NetWeaver AS ABAP and ABAP Platform, SAP Commerce Cloud, SAP Data Hub, and NetWeaver Application Server Java, with no exploitation reported for those listed issues. CISA added Cisco, Chrome, and Arista vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The additions included CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, CVE-2026-11645 in Google Chrome V8, and CVE-2026-7473 in Arista EOS. Arista stated it does not plan to patch CVE-2026-7473 due to potential configuration breakage and instead documented ACL-based mitigations. Black Lotus Labs reporting, covered by both The Hacker News and BleepingComputer, described the China-linked JDY botnet as having expanded to more than 1,500 compromised SOHO and IoT devices. The botnet is used as a centrally controlled reconnaissance network for service discovery, banner grabbing, TLS certificate collection, protocol fingerprinting, and flaw-focused scanning after public vulnerability disclosures. The botnet has expanded beyond earlier Cisco RV320 and RV325 router activity to include devices from Araknis, Mimosa Networks, Ubiquiti, DrayTek, Hikvision, Linksys, and Cisco. Researchers said JDY places strong emphasis on U.S. targets, including U.S. military and associated networks. Command infrastructure reportedly uses Tor hidden services, and some activity involves the open-source Platypus reverse-shell and host-management framework. A cyberattack disrupted Mackay Sugar operations in Queensland, forcing shutdowns at the Farleigh and Racecourse mills and prompting growers to stop harvesting sugarcane until further communication. The company said it engaged cybersecurity experts and local authorities, implemented temporary measures to keep essential operations running, and was working to restore affected systems. Mackay Sugar did not disclose whether ransomware, data theft, or another form of malicious activity was involved. Identity and access management remained a parallel theme, particularly around third parties and account verification. Health-ISAC released a white paper on third-party IAM risks in healthcare, emphasizing vendor, partner, remote access, connected device, and facility management complexity. Separate reporting on identity verification cited a 160% surge in credential theft in 2025 and described phishing-resistant MFA, service desk verification, device trust signals, and passkeys as major areas of focus in modern identity programs.

item.title: CVE-2026-42897, CVE-2026-45657, and AI-Enabled Phishing Drive June Threat Coverage
item.description: This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with...
item.content:encoded: This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with AI-generated sites, adversary-in-the-middle kits, event-themed fraud, and low-content email payloads complicating detection. Microsoft patched CVE-2026-42897, an actively exploited high-severity spoofing vulnerability in Exchange Server affecting Exchange Server 2016, Exchange Server 2019, and Exchange Server Subscription Edition. The flaw can be exploited remotely without privileges by sending a specially crafted email that, under certain Outlook Web Access interaction conditions, executes arbitrary JavaScript in the user’s browser context. CISA added the vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 15 and set a May 29 remediation deadline for U.S. federal agencies. Microsoft’s June Patch Tuesday fixed more than 200 security flaws, described by multiple vulnerability trackers as the largest monthly release in the program’s history. Among the most severe was CVE-2026-45657, a critical Windows kernel vulnerability rated 9.8 that could allow remote full system compromise without user interaction and was characterized by ZDI as wormable. The release also included CVE-2026-41091, an actively exploited Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege flaw that could allow an attacker with an existing foothold to gain full control of a system. The same Microsoft coverage included three disclosed zero-days, including CVE-2026-50507, a BitLocker bypass tied to the pseudonymous researcher Nightmare Eclipse. Separately, Microsoft reported that a small percentage of devices upgraded to Windows 11 versions 24H2 or 25H2 may fail to install the latest cumulative update, with errors including 0x80073712 and 0x800f0993 and logs referencing component store corruption or missing hydration candidates. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported that phishing activity declined by roughly 20% year over year in both 2024 and 2025, while attackers shifted toward more targeted and personalized lures resembling routine business workflows. The report identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances, with 37,447, or 9.06%, flagged as malicious, and attributed builders including Manus AI, Blackbox AI, and Anything AI. ThreatLabz also found that 95.2% of phishing activity was delivered over encrypted channels. The same research described phishing kits using adversary-in-the-middle and browser-in-the-middle techniques to capture credentials and MFA codes during live login flows, enabling session-level compromise rather than simple password theft. ThreatLabz also recorded 89.9 million hostile interactions with external decoys over six months and observed more than 121,000 distinct AWS-hosted IPs probing customer environments, underscoring the scale of opportunistic reconnaissance against exposed infrastructure. Event-themed fraud is already appearing around the FIFA World Cup 2026. The FBI warned that threat actors are creating fraudulent FIFA-affiliated websites to collect personal data, conduct financial fraud, and sell fake goods or services, while Cyble confirmed that many FBI-flagged domains remained active at publication time. Reported examples included ww-fifa[.]com, a fake hospitality and ticket portal requesting personal and payment details, and fifaworldcup-careers[.]com, a fake recruitment domain associated with multiple IP addresses and wildcard SSL certificates. IRONSCALES analyzed an “ACH payment” email sent from carlaburbo@keystonenatural[.]com to an accounts receivable team, with ap@keystonenatural[.]com copied. The message passed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and composite authentication at final delivery, while original authentication results showed dkim=none and dmarc=none, and ARC validation at i=2 returned cv=fail, indicating a broken forwarding chain of trust. The email’s payload consisted entirely of a single PNG attachment named image002.png, with no message body, no links, and no OCR-extracted text. Themis flagged the message at 54% confidence as credential theft, and one mailbox was quarantined, illustrating how image-only payloads and authentication-chain inconsistencies can coexist with apparently successful final-delivery authentication outcomes. A related control-validation theme appeared in reporting on automated pentesting limitations. The article argued that clean automated pentest results can create false confidence because they show exploitable paths but not whether SIEM rules fired, EDR alerts were raised, or SOC analysts had sufficient signal to act. It contrasted automated pentesting with breach and attack simulation, framing the latter as focused on whether controls blocked, detected, logged, or missed known malicious behaviors.
